Risking his own life, he ran towards his friend with utmost speed, caught hold of the boy and jumped on to the footpath, saving the friend from certain death. It all happened in the twinkling of an eye and the bystanders profusely praised the boldness and presence of mind of child Naren.
speaker: Unknown

title: Testing the Caste Pipes
content: Naren’s father Viswanath Dutta was a flourishing Attorney of the Calcutta High Court and among his clients, there were Muslims, Christians, Brahmins, non-brahmins and others. In order to entertain them, tobacco pipes (Hukka) were provided separately for each caste. Narendra used to notice carefully that each one of the client was using the tobacco pipe marked for a particular caste. It was a riddle to him. Why this distinction? What would happen if a Muslim would smoke from a Brahmin’s pipe or vice-versa? And because some elders had threatened him once that one would lose one’s caste if one would smoke from the other’s pipe, he wanted to test it for himself. So one day when the father was busy talking to the clients outside and nobody was there in the room, young Naren made the circuit of the pipes taking a whiff from every pipe including the one meant for the Mohammedans. But nothing happened, he did not find any change in him. The father who had entered at that juncture was amused to see Naren near the pipes and asked him what he was doing there. Narendra replied, ‘I am testing whether I would lose my caste by smoking every pipe. But nothing has happened to me father!' and started laughing.
speaker: Unknown

title: The Brahmadaitya Ghost
content: During holidays, he would sometimes go to the neighbouring house along with a friend, climb a big flowering tree and would enjoy swinging like a bat with his head downwards catching hold of a branch with the legs. The grandfather of the house got afraid seeing young Naren so swinging, and ordered him to come down and never to climb that tree again. Narendra asked, ‘What will happen if I climb?’ The grandfather in order to frighten him said, ‘There is a terrible Brahmadaitya (Brahmin’s ghost) living on that tree and if you climb on it, it will break your neck’. Naren listened politely but as soon as the old man was out of sight, he climbed up the tree again. His friend was terribly afraid to hear about the ghost but Naren laughed at him saying, ‘What an ass you are! My neck would have been off long ago, if there were really a ghost. Don't you understand that the grandfather has only threatened us so that we might not get on the tree again?’
speaker: Unknown

title: Narendra Refuses the Supernatural Powers
content: Once Sri Ramakrishna asked Narendranath (Swami Vivekananda) : “My child as the result of practising austerities I have got all the supernatural powers, such as assuming the minute dimension of an atom etc. But I have no use for them. I am now thinking of asking the Mother to transfer all these to you. For She has told me that you will have to do much of Her work. If all these powers are imparted to you, you will be able to use them when necessary. What do you say?’ Narendra put the counter question to the Master, ‘Sir, will they help me in realizing God?’ The Master replied, ‘They might be of no help to you in that respect, but they stand you in good stead when you engage yourself in God’s work after realizing Him.’ Even then Narendra’s reply was, ‘Sir, I have no need of these things. Let me realize God first and then it will be decided whether to accept them or not.’ The Master was testing Narendra by tempting him as it were but when the disciple stood the test, the Master was highly pleased.
speaker: Unknown

title: The Aim of Life and the Banyan Tree
content: Once Sri Ramakrishna asked Swami Vivekananda, who was then young Narendranath Datta, about the aim of his life, to which he replied: ‘I shall like to remain always immersed in Nirvikalpa Samadhi and enjoy the divine bliss.’ At once came the mild retort from Sri Ramakrishna, who said: “I can never imagine that you will be so small and selfish only to enjoy yourself. You should on the other hand, be like a big banyan tree, under whose cooling shade thousands of weary souls will take shelter and be cooled and consoled.” The tree not only stands the scorching heat or biting cold or heavy rain and violent storm, but it always welcomes others irrespective of any distinction and offers them its soothing shade.
speaker: Unknown

title: It Is Not Mercy but Service
content: On another occasion, Sri Ramakrishna was sitting on his cot in his room at Dakshineswar in a half-ecstatic mood surrounded by Narendranath and others when all of a sudden he said, ‘It is not mercy but service, it is to serve jiva (being) as the veritable manifestation of Lord Siva.’ Narendranath came out of the room and told others: ‘I have heard something new to-day and if it be the will of God I shall try to give shape to what I have heard.’
speaker: Unknown

title: The Soft Bed and the Poor Countrymen
content: Once, he was the guest of a rich lady there who arranged a very soft bed on a costly and cosy spring cot. At the dead of night, the lady heard some sound of sobbing from Swamiji's room. She came running to see with great sorrow and bewilderment that the Swami was rolling on the ground and weeping like a child. On being asked the reason, the Swami said: 'As soon as I lay down on the soft bed so kindly provided by you, at once the thought of my poor countrymen flashed before my eyes. They don't even get a mat for a bed, or even a brick for their pillow. You are my flesh and blood and when they suffer like this, how could I sleep on a comfortable bed? Their pitiable plight troubled my mind so much.'
speaker: Unknown

title: Love for India Before and After
content: After the most successful and triumphant tour in the West where Swamiji was showered with all love and reverence and where the most aristocratic society deemed it a great honour to receive Swami Vivekananda, on the eve of his departure for India, he was asked by one of his devoted disciples, 'Swamiji, after passing these four years in the company of the cream of society of this country and receiving so much of love, adoration and affection and seeing for yourself the tip-top and advanced condition of this country, how will you like India which is steeped in deep ignorance and full of abominable customs and manners?' At once came the bold reply from the patriotic Swami, 'India I loved before I came; now the very dust of India has become holy to me, the very air is now to me holy, India now the holy land, the place of pilgrimage, the Tirtha, and I am eagerly waiting for the blessed day when I shall be able to roll myself on the sacred dust of India.'
speaker: Unknown

title: Guru Govinda Singh and the Bracelets
content:
There is a story about Guru Govinda Singh, the great spiritual leader of the Sikhs, and a rich disciple of his.

Guru Govinda Singh was once sitting on the banks of the Jumna saying his prayers. It was evening. Raghunath, a rich disciple, came and bowed down, saying: "Sir, pray accept this trifling present in token of my love." So saying, he laid at the feet of the Master two gold bracelets, inlaid with rare gems. The Guru, or the teacher, accepted the ornaments, and as if to display his pleasure, he began to play with one of the bracelets, tossing it into the air and catching it in his palm. Suddenly he let the bracelet slip and fall into the river.

Raghunath, the disciple, took it to be a sad accident; he jumped into the river to recover it. He continued to search for it, while the teacher all the while remained absorbed in meditation. Late in the evening Raghunath returned from his futile search with downcast eyes. He said: "Master, I am sorry, I have failed to find the jewel so far, but I can possibly still get it if you will only point out the exact spot where it fell."

Knowing as he did all that passed in the mind of his disciple, the Guru or the teacher took the other bracelet and threw that too in the river saying: "Raghunath, it was just there!"

Raghunath stood stunned and bewildered at this deliberate act of the teacher. He was unable to divine the Master's meaning in casting away the second ornament also. But after a few moments the teacher rose from his seat and embracing his disciple warmly said: "Raghunath, I got rid of the bracelets on purpose. I saw how your mind is attached to them and they were a screen between you and me. Get rid of your pride of wealth."

Raghunath now realized his folly. He fell at the feet of the Master and from that moment on was a changed man!
speaker: Unknown

title: The Lord as the Mother
content: The story goes that when a young Vaisya woman who was an ardent devotee of Siva was looking forward to the help of her mother during her confinement and the mother was held up by floods in the Cauvery, the Lord Himself came to the devotee in the guise of her mother and thenceforth He came to be known as Matri-Bhuteswara or Thayumanavar.
speaker: Unknown

title: Narendra's First Visit to the Master
content:
Sri Ramakrishna told us once briefly his thoughts about Narendra, when he had come to him for the first time. He said: 'Narendra entered this room and I noticed that he took no care of his body, his hair and dress were not at all trim; unlike all others, he had no desire at all for any external object. He was, as it were, unattached to anything. His eyes indicated that a major part of his mind was perforce ever indrawn.'

Narendra, on his part, described his first visit to the Master thus: 'After I finished singing, the Master suddenly got up and, taking me by the hand, led me to the northern verandah and closed the door. Then he caught hold of my hand and shed profuse tears of joy. Addressing me affectionately, like one already familiar, he said: "Is it proper that you should come so late? Should you not have once thought, how I was waiting for you? Hearing continually the idle talk of worldly people, my ears are about to be scorched. Not having any one to communicate my innermost feelings to, I am about to burst." And so he went on raving and weeping. The next moment he stood before me with folded palms and, showing me the regard due to a god, went on saying: "I know, my Lord, thou art that ancient Rishi Nara, a part of Narayana, who has incarnated himself this time, to remove the miseries and sufferings of humanity...." The next moment he entered the room and brought some butter, candy and sandes; with his own hand he fed me all the sweets before he could rest content. Taking my hand he said: "Promise, you will soon come again and all alone." Re-entering the room with him, I sat down beside my companions and went on observing him closely. Impressed by his fine talk and Bhavasamadhi, I thought, he is truly a man of renunciation who has given up his all for God and practises personally what he says: God can be seen and spoken with, just as I am seeing you and speaking with you. Speechless I thought, "well, he may be mad, but how pure! and what renunciation!"'
speaker: Unknown

title: Nandanar's Trial by Fire
content:
The name of Nandanar is well-known in Tamil-nad. He belonged to the ‘ out-caste’ section of the Hindu community. He hailed from Adaniir, on the bank of the Coleroon in Tanjore District. From his birth he was blessed with God-consciousness. He would never, even forgetfully, worship any one other than Siva as his Lord. Hence he lived a righteous life, dedicated to the service of the Lord. He accepted the limitations which birth in a ‘low caste’ imposed on him; but he turned them to good account by converting his work into worship. He supplied leather and other straps to the temple for making drums, strings for various kinds of lute and bezoar for worship of the Lord. So, in all possible ways consistent with his limitations, he served the Lord. Standing outside the temple-precincts, he would sing and dance in joy evoked by his love for his Lord.

Nandanar went to Tiruppunkir for worship. But could not get a good view of the Lord’s image because of the sacred bull (Nandi) at the entrance. The Lord answered his prayer, to ask Nandi to move aside so that Nandanar could have a good view. Nandanar dug a tank before going back to his village. He worshipped Siva in various other places. Once he was seized with a desire to worship at Tillai (Chidambaram). This desire made him sleepless. He realized, however, that because of his ‘low-caste’ birth, this desire could not be fulfilled and started consoling himself by saying ‘I shall go tomorrow’. (Hence his name ‘ Tirunalaippovar’).

Not being satisfied with this way of postponing it to the morrow, Nandanar set out for Chidambaram. When he drew near Chidambaram, he saw smoke issuing from the sacrificial fires tended by the Brahmins. He considered the place too sacred to enter and started going round and round the walls of the city. His thought by day and dream by night were about worshipping the Lord in the temple. How was he to do it? While he was asleep with sheer exhaustion, the Lord appeared to him in a dream. Smiling, He spoke to him these words: ‘To be rid of this birth, bathe in fire and come to me with the Brahmins.’ The Lord commanded likewise the Brahmins to prepare a fire for Nandanar. The Brahmins were awe-struck but proceeded to carry out the Lord’s behest. Addressing Nandanar as a Brahmin they told him of the Lord’s command. Nandanar felt assured of his salvation. The fire was lit near the southern wall. After going round the fire prayerfully, Nandanar entered it. Lo and behold! he came out of it as a sage, with sacred thread and matted hair. His ‘low birth’ was gone and he attained sainthood. The Brahmins and other devotees gathered there were filled with joy.

Nandanar was led into the temple. But even as he entered it in devotion, he disappeared from view.
speaker: Unknown

title: The Irish Colonists in America
content: Swamiji used to give the example of the Irish Colonists in America. When they first came to America they were down-hearted, haggard-looking, destitute of all possessions. But after six months they presented a different spectacle. ‘The same man now walks upright, is attire has changed, his look has become bold and fearless now. Why so much of change ? . . . because in Ireland he was kept surrounded by contempt and he was always told, “ Pat, you have no more hope, you are born a slave and will remain a slave,” believing this he had hypnotised himself, but as soon as he came to America, he was told, “ Pat, you are a man, as we are men...a man like you and me can do everything ; have a ... Pat raised his head and saw that it was so. . . and so the transformation.’
speaker: Swami Vivekananda

title: The Cloth and Maya
content: One day when Sri Ramakrishna was talking with the devotees he alluded to the All-pervading God's nearness and said though God is very near to us, we cannot see him because of the veiling power of Maya. And to illustrate he said, he suddenly held up a piece of cloth between himself and the audience, 'You cannot see me now though I am so near! Maya too covers God.'
speaker: Sri Ramakrishna

title: The Tank Covered with Scum
content: On another occasion he gave the instance of a tank covered with scum. Let us quote his own words: 'When I was explaining God's actions to someone, God suddenly showed me the tank at Kamarpukur. I saw a man removing the green scum and drinking the water. The water was clear as crystal. God revealed to me that Satchidananda is covered by the scum of ignorance. He who puts the green scum aside can get the water.'
speaker: Sri Ramakrishna

title: Sashi Throws His Persian Books into the Ganges
content: It is said that once coming to know of the excellence of Sufi Poetry he began studying Persian. He took to its study so seriously that even when he went to Dakshineswar he used to carry those books with him. One day he was so absorbed in his study that he did not hear Sri Ramakrishna call until the Master had called him thrice. When, however, the Master observed, 'If you forget your duties for the sake of particular studies, you will lose your devotion,' Sashi made a bundle of those books and threw them into the Ganges. Books lost all importance for him from then onwards.
speaker: Unknown

title: Sankara and the Crocodile
content: The path of renunciation appealed to him most. In the life of Sankara, we have the strange episode as to how he came to take sannyasa. One morning when mother and son went to the river for bathing, a crocodile caught hold of the latter. The boy Sankara cried out to his mother, asking her permission to renounce the world and take sannyasa. The crocodile released Sankara.
speaker: Unknown

title: The Jar of Nectar
content: On one occasion Sri Ramakrishna asked Narendranath: 'Suppose you had a jar of nectar, what would you do?' Narendranath replied: 'I would sit on the edge of the vessel and drink it. If I venture further I would fall over and die.' 'No, my boy, you will not die. It is the Ocean of nectar, of immortality. One becomes immortal by diving into it. Dive and drink deep of it.'
speaker: Sri Ramakrishna

title: The Raindrop and the Ocean
content: There was once a rain drop which fell into the ocean and it began to weep. The ocean asked the rain drop what ailed it. It said I am losing my identity. The ocean laughed and said, "No, my dear, you are becoming one with your brothers and sisters here. But if, however, you find your life miserable rise above through the sun's rays and travel as you wish, but then you will have to face the rugged mountains and such other hardships."
speaker: Swami Vivekananda

title: The Dispute Between Brahma and Vishnu
content: The Skanda Purana contains an episode according to which there arose a dispute between Brahma and Vishnu. Each claimed superiority over the other, resulting in a fierce duel. In the meanwhile, Siva interposed in the form of a fiery pillar and said that he who correctly gauged the two ends of that form was decidedly superior to the other. While Brahma in the form of a swan flew up to the aerial regions to find out the top, Vishnu assumed the form of a boar and burrowed down deep into the earth to find its bottom. However, all their efforts were in vain and they had to admit defeat.
speaker: Unknown

title: Divine Sport for the Sake of Humanity
content: Narendra came and sat down beside Sri Ramakrishna in his room at Dakshineswar, at about four in the afternoon. As soon as the Master saw him, he entered into Bhavasamadhi and sat close to him almost on his lap, saying: 'What I see is, that this (his body) am I, and this one (Narendra's) too am I. Verily I say, I see no difference. Just as the water of the Ganga seems to be divided in two parts when a stick is placed on it, but actually there are no divisions; there exists but one whole mass of water; so it is here. Do you understand? What exists but Mother? What do you say?' Then he suddenly said: 'I shall smoke.' After a puff or two he returned the hookah, saying: 'I will smoke with the bowl' took it in his hands and began smoking. After a few puffs he held it near Narendra's mouth and said: 'Just have a puff, a puff through my hands.' Narendra shrank, at which the Master said: 'What are you afraid of? You and I are different? This am I, that too am I' He then held his hands in front of Narendra's mouth to make him smoke. Thus compelled, Narendra smoked two or three puffs through the Master's hands and then stopped. Seeing him stop smoking, Sri Ramakrishna himself washed his hands and then said something, but the Master paid no heed to these words. He said: 'You wretch, you are awful ...' But the Master paid no heed to it. Narendra was conscious of a hookah a portion of which had been given to him to smoke, and he could not bring himself practically to touch the bowl with his lips as it had been touched by the other's mouth. In this case, however, he smoked with his hands over his own mouth, which showed how near to his heart Narendra was.
speaker: Unknown

title: Laltoo's Vision of Paramahansa
content: I repeat, 'No, you do not know, Paramahansa Masay is definitely here.' At this firm faith of the boy I kept quiet and went away to attend to the evening prayer in the temple. Coming to the temple I remembered that I did not give Laltoo the consecrated food. So I took some prasada and returned to him. When I went there I found him bowing down by touching his forehead to the ground. Mystified I kept quiet. After a couple of minutes when the boy saw me standing in front of him, he was surprised and asked me 'Ah! where has Paramahansa Masay gone?' Taken aback I could not answer. I handed over the prasada and came away to the temple.
speaker: Swami Adbhutananda

title: The Genesis of the Ramayana
content: The way the epic came to be was wonderful. Valmiki, the great sage. Once gone to a river for his bath, he saw a pair of flamingoes on the top of a tree. A hunter shot the male bird. The sage, moved by pity, cursed the hunter. But Brahma, the creator, appeared before the Rishi, pacified him and said that he would do immense good to the world, if he composed the life of Sri Rama, as already related to him by Narada, in this poetical strain. Since then bards have sung this poem, pundits have extolled its greatness, folk songs have grown round the characters of the Ramayana so much so that it has penetrated into the very hearts of our people.
speaker: Unknown

title: The Weaver's Unshakable Faith
content: Look here. There lived a weaver in a village. He was pious, people loved him and had great faith in him. He used to sell cloth in the market. When a purchaser came and asked him about the price of a cloth, he used to say: It is Rama’s (Lord’s) will, (that) the cost of the yarn is so much. Rama’s will, that of labour is this much; Rama’s will, my profit is this much. Such was the faith of the people in him that they would at once pay the price and take away the cloth. One day it so happened that at night as he was praying to the Lord, taking His name, a gang of robbers, on their way to commit robbery, forced him to accompany them to serve as a porter. As they were returning with the load on the weaver’s head, they were encountered by the police. All fled away except the weaver with the load. The poor man had to bear all kinds of humiliation at the hands of the police. But the weaver did not give up saying, ‘Rama’s will’. He was hauled up in a court of justice. When he was asked to narrate his case—he said, ‘My Lord, by Rama’s will, I was taking God’s name. By Rama’s will robbers came and forced me to accompany them. By Rama’s will, they committed robbery. By Rama’s will, they placed the load on my head. By Rama’s will, we all started. By Rama’s will, the police came. By Rama’s will robbers fled. By Rama’s will, I was caught. By Rama’s will, I was subjected to humiliation. By Rama’s will I am produced before your Lordship.’ The judge understood the situation and set the weaver free. While leaving the court weaver said, ‘By Rama’s will, I am set free.’
speaker: Unknown

title: Indra Steals Prahlada's Virtuous Conduct
content:
Dhrtarastra then told a story which was narrated by Narada in the days of yore in this context:

"Prahlada by his virtuous conduct conquered the three worlds. At this Indra was greatly grieved. He went to the Guru of the devas, Brhaspati, and wanted to know how happiness could be had. Brhaspati said that knowledge alone could bring supreme good. Indra again asked whether there was anything superior to it.

Brhaspati then directed Indra to Sukracarya, who he said was more proficient than himself. Indra learnt whatever Sukracarya had to teach and again asked whether there was anything higher than what he had taught. Sukracarya then sent Indra to Prahlada.

Indra took the form of a Brahmana and approached Prahlada to be taught the way to highest felicity. Prahlada said that he was fully occupied with the governing of the three worlds and had no time to teach him.

But Indra was inexorable. He said he would wait and would be glad to be taught whenever Prahlada could find leisure. Prahlada was pleased with the earnestness of the pupil. When the proper time arrived he gladly imparted knowledge. Indra in the Brahmana guise whole-heartedly served the teacher as a disciple bound to do. He questioned him several ways as to how he attained the lordship of the three worlds. Prahlada said that the secret of his success was that virtuous conduct (Sila) was considered the greatest gift one could possess. It could be learnt from any person, and it was also the tradition that if the candidate was found fit and if he approached in the proper ways, the disciple was to be taught even if one had to go to the enemy camp. Sukracarya was the youngest son of the demoniac race and had annexed the kingdom of Indra and subordinated him.

Humility, self-control, eagerness to listen to the counsels of the wise and respect to elders. After a time Prahlada who was pleased with the attendance of his disciple asked him to beg a boon of him.

The disciple said: 'O King, if you are pleased with me and if you want to do something agreeable to me please grant me that your virtuous conduct may be mine, this is the only boon I covet.' Prahlada was puzzled at this strange request of the Brahmana and thought this disciple was no ordinary man. He had strange misgivings but as he had promised, he granted the boon. The Brahmana receiving the boon went away. Soon Prahlada was filled with grief and deep anxiety. As he was thus brooding over the incident a great light in the form of a flame came out of his body. Prahlada asked who he was; to which the latter replied that he was virtuous conduct whom he had discarded and was going to reside in the Brahmana to whom he had given him.

Replying in this way the light vanished and went in search of the Brahmana. As soon as this powerful light had disappeared another figure similar in brilliance came out of Prahlada's body. On being questioned who he was, the shining figure replied that he was Dharma (righteousness) and that he was going away to the foremost of the Brahmanas as he could not stay separated from virtuous conduct (Sila). A third luminous person which called himself Truth (satya) by and by followed Dharma; him followed a fourth one which styled himself as 'Mode of life' (vrtta). Again a fifth called strength left Prahlada's body and lastly Sri, the goddess of wealth too was leaving him when Prahlada asked who that Brahmana was that could thus deplete him of all his possessions. Sri said that the Brahmana who had served him so diligently was no other than Indra who had come to steal away his virtues. She further apprised Prahlada that he had held sovereignty over the three worlds only due to his virtuous conduct. Indra coming to know of this secret had taken it away from him.

Thus Dharma, truth, mode of life, strength and wealth have virtuous conduct as their root (Silamula). In this there is no doubt, O Sri, the goddess of wealth."

When Prahlada had related this story, Duryodhana asked him to tell him the ways of acquiring this sila. Dhrtarastra said: "Listen, O King. What has been taught in great detail by Prahlada I shall tell you in brief. Freedom from malice towards all beings in mind, word and deed, compassion and charity comprise the best parts of virtuous conduct (Sila). It also constitutes in not doing such actions that hurt others or cause starvation to others. One should undertake only such actions for which one will receive approbation of a court of wise men. This in short is virtuous conduct."
speaker: Unknown

title: Nag Mahashaya Refuses to Eat
content: Myself, Swami Turiyananda and I appeared at Nag Mahashaya's cottage. Seeing us he rose politely. We said, "We shall have our meal here today?" At once Nag Mahashaya brought rice, cooking pot and fuel from the bazar and began to cook eagerly that we would eat and make Nag Mahashaya also eat. Cooking over, he gave to us; we set apart something for him and sat down to eat. After this, we requested him to take food; he at once broke the pot and striking his forehead began to say, "I give food to the body in which God has been realized?" Seeing this we were filled with amazement. Later on, after much persuasion we induced him to take some food and then returned.
speaker: Swami Vivekananda

title: The Cossipore Garden Revelation
content: One day while he was staying at the Cossipore garden, his body in imminent danger of falling off for ever, by the side of his bed I was saying in my mind, ‘Well, now if you can declare that you are God, then only will I believe you are really God Himself.’ It was only two days before he passed away. Immediately, he looked up towards me all on a sudden and said, ‘He who was Rama, He who was Krishna, verily is He now Ramakrishna in this body. And that not merely from the standpoint of your Vedanta!’ At this I was struck dumb.
speaker: Swami Vivekananda

title: The Nose-Screw That Changed a Miser
content:
His profession hoarded a good amount of wealth. His miserliness increased in proportion to the increase in his wealth. He did not know what charity was.

A certain old Brahmin approached him for financial help to meet the expenses of the upanayana (ceremony of investiture of the sacred thread). Krishnappa turned a deaf ear to the request. The Brahmin did not give up. He went on calling on Krishnappa for aid. But, seeing no help was forthcoming, the Brahmin approached Krishnappa's wife for the purpose.

She was much moved by the predicament. She decided to help the cause. As she had no money of her own, she thought it would be really helpful if she were to part with her diamond nose-screw. She whole-heartedly gave the nose-screw to the Brahmin with the words 'Sri Krishnarpanamasty' (dedicated to Sri Krishna).

The old Brahmin came to Krishnappa Naik and asked him to give him money in lieu of the diamond nose-screw. Krishnappa was shocked to see the old man with a costly ornament. His curiosity was roused when he found the nose-screw bore a close resemblance to the similar ornament of his wife. He kept the ornament and gave some money, which, of course, was far less than the actual value of the ornament, and was happy.

He hurriedly came home and found the ornament of his wife missing. He enquired about it. She was not courageous enough to confess to her miserly husband about her charity. She said that she had mislaid it and would search for it. She was perturbed and in that peculiar state of her mind, she resolved to commit suicide by taking poison. She offered her last prayers to the Lord. But when she took the cup of poison into her hands and brought it near her lips she heard a voice as a gift in the cup. She at once ran to her husband and showed him the nose-screw. He felt happy and rushed to his cupboard. He found that the ornament which he purchased from the Brahmin was not there. In its place it was kept in the safe under lock and key.

This incident was the turning point in Krishnappa's life. He ascertained all the details from his devoted wife. In the meanwhile, the servant who was deputed to follow the Brahmin, returned with the message that the Brahmin had disappeared in the Vithoba Temple. Krishnappa felt humbled. The Divine Light in him began to glow. Soon he gave away all his wealth in charity and solemnly resolved to lead a holy life. The miser turned a master munificent.
speaker: Unknown

title: The Light of the Self
content:
There was once a discussion between Emperor Janaka and Sage Yajnavalkya regarding the Vedic rite known as Agnihotra wherein the former displayed an admirable grasp of the Subject. Being pleased with the Emperor's erudition, the sage conferred a boon on him. Janaka wanted the privilege of addressing any question to Yajnavalkya and having the benefit of his considered views on that subject. 'Be it so,' said the sage.

On a later occasion, when Yajnavalkya met Janaka in his Court, the latter exercised his privilege and asked the question relating to the light which served man in all his activities.

Janaka: O Yajnavalkya, what serves as the light for a man?

Yajnavalkya: The light of the sun, O Emperor. It is through the light of the sun that he sits, goes out, works and returns.

Janaka: It is just so. When, however, the sun has set, what serves as the light for a man?

Yajnavalkya: The moon serves as his light.

Janaka: That is true, but when the sun and the moon have both set, what serves as the light for a man?

Yajnavalkya: The fire serves as his light.

Janaka: What you say is true, but when the sun and the moon have both set and the fire has gone out, what serves as the light for a man?

Yajnavalkya: Speech serves as his light. (In utter darkness) when even one's own hand is not clearly visible, if a sound is heard, one manages to go to that place.

Janaka: Quite true, but when the sun and the moon have both set, the fire has gone out and speech is stopped, what serves as the light for a man?

Yajnavalkya: Then the Self serves as his light. It is through the light of the Self that he sits, goes out, works and returns.

Janaka: That is exactly so, but which is the Self?
speaker: Unknown

title: The Cowboy Test of Vivekananda
content: Hearing him speak of Indian philosophy, a number of university men, who had become cowboys took him at his word when he said that only one who had realised the Highest was equanimous under all conditions and was not disturbed by any outward influences. They decided to put him to the test and so invited him to lecture to them. When he arrived they escorted him to a wooden tub which they had placed with the bottom up to serve as a platform in the public square of their village. The Swami commenced his address, soon losing himself in the subject. Suddenly there was a deafening noise of shots which went whizzing past his ears! Undisturbed he continued his lecture to the end as though nothing was happening.
speaker: Unknown

title: Indra and Virochana Seek the Atman
content:
Once Prajapati (the Creator) announced, ‘the Atman, which is untouched by impurity, devoid of old age, deathless, griefless, not liable to hunger and thirst, whose desires come true, whose thoughts come true, is to be sought after, is to be Known. One who understands Having been taught (by a teacher), obtains all the worlds and all desires.’

Hearing about it Indra among the gods and Virochana among the demons approached Prajapati with due respect and after living for some time and serving Him requested Him to teach them that highest knowledge. Prajapati said : ‘The Purusa that is seen in the eye that is the Atman. This is immortal, fearless. This is Brahman.’ They asked which was the Atman, that which was reflected in the mirror or that which was reflected in water. Prajapati first asked them to see as they were in water and again after adorning themselves with ornaments etc. Being still not of the required purity of mind, they could not assess the meaning of these instructions of Prajapati. Describing the reflection each time they asked whether that was the Atman that he meant. Prajapati only repeated his previous formula ‘This is the Atman, This is Immortal, fearless. This is Brahman.’

Pleased at heart both of them went away thinking that they had known all. Prajapati seeing them go away satisfied said: ‘They are going away without understanding the Self. But whoever goes away, whether gods or demons, without understanding this knowledge will perish.’

Of the two Virochana firmly believed that what Prajapati meant by Atman was the body, and went away perfectly satisfied and taught his followers to build up their bodies. But Indra being a little more thoughtful doubted this doctrine and approaching again, learnt the real import of the teaching. Now who was responsible for Virochana’s fault? His own defectiveness, lack of introspectiveness.
speaker: Unknown

title: The Master's Final Gift to Naren
content: caressing them lovingly, speaking eloquently by means of signs of the love he bore them. His constant thought was, ‘What will become of them without me?’ But there was Naren! Naren had been called to the side of the Master some days earlier, when he was suffering intensely and could scarcely speak. The Master wrote on a piece of paper, ‘Narendra will teach others.’ Naren hesitated and said, ‘I won't do that.’ But the Master replied, ‘You shall have to do it.’ It was now only three or four days before the Master’s Mahasamadhi. Sri Ramakrishna called Naren to him. Looking steadfastly at him he entered into deep meditation. Naren felt as if a subtle force, resembling an electric shock, were entering his body, and he lost all outer consciousness. When he came to, he found the Master weeping. Wondering, Naren asked Sri Ramakrishna why he wept, and was told, ‘Oh Naren, today I have given you my all and have become a Fakir, a penniless beggar. By the force of the power transmitted by me, great things will be done by you; only after that will you go to whence you came.’ A couple of days before the final Mahasamadhi of the Master, as Naren was standing by his bedside, a curious thought flashed across his mind. He said many times that he is an Incarnation of God. If I can make him say now as he is in the throes of death, in the midst of human anguish and physical pain, “I am God incarnate”, then I will believe him!’ The moment this thought came to him, the Master turned towards him and summoning all his energy said distinctly. ‘O my Naren, are you not yet convinced? He who is Rama and Krishna is now Ramakrishna in this body — but not from the point of your Vedanta!’
speaker: Unknown

title: The Humble Salutation of Girish Babu
content:
One day, a rich devotee of Latu Maharaj saluted him, as is usually done in Bengal, by raising and joining the two hands above his head. At this Latu Maharaj said, ‘Look here, brother, one should bow down to Sadhus and Sannyasis and to gods and goddesses. He (the Master) used to say, “This sort of axe-raising salutation brings no merit.”’

Another devotee present on the occasion was amused at this and asked with a laugh, ‘What is this “axe-raising salutation”, Maharaj?’

Latu Maharaj : ‘The kind of salutation that you usually do by touching the forehead with joined and upraised hands is what he termed “axe-raising” salutation. One day Girish Babu saluted Sri Ramakrishna thus. Immediately the Master bent himself at his waist and saluted Girish. We were witnessing the scene. At this Girish bent himself lower and saluted. The Master lowered himself still more. This competition ended when Girish fell flat on the ground and bowed to the Master. Then Sri Ramakrishna blessed him. It is for this Girish Babu used to say, “This time the Master conquered the world with humble salutations.”’
speaker: Unknown
